currentQuizCards.name: Ace of Cups
currentQuizCards.upright: A hand emerges from a cloud bearing a cup overflowing with water — five streams, a dove descending, a lotus floating. The Ace of Cups is the seed of emotional experience: new love, compassion, creative inspiration, spiritual connection. Whatever emotional beginning is possible, it is here. The question is whether you are open to receiving it.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Emotional repression, a blocked heart, or the beginning that cannot be received. Something is offered and refused — consciously or not.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Lovers: deep romantic potential arriving. Next to The Two of Cups: a connection about to become real. Next to The High Priestess: trust what you feel before you understand it.

scenario.love: A new relationship, or the renewal of genuine feeling in an existing one.
scenario.work: Creative work flowing from real passion rather than obligation — something that actually means something.
scenario.self: What would it mean to let yourself feel this fully, without managing it?

currentQuizCards.name: Seven of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: A figure steals away from a camp carrying five swords, glancing back with a sly expression. Two swords are left behind. The Seven of Swords speaks to deception, to the strategy that avoids direct confrontation, to something being taken or withheld. In a reading it asks whether someone (yourself or another) is being fully honest, and what the evasion is protecting.
currentQuizCards.reversed: The truth coming out, or excessive suspicion where none is warranted.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Moon: deception wrapped in confusion. Next to The Devil: manipulativeness as a pattern. Next to Justice: the evasion catching up with someone.

scenario.love: Dishonesty in a relationship — from a partner, or from yourself about your own intentions.
scenario.work: Office politics, someone taking credit, or a strategy that relies on others not knowing.
scenario.self: What are you not being fully honest about — with yourself or someone else?

currentQuizCards.name: Nine of Wands
currentQuizCards.upright: A battered figure leans on a wand, one more wand to raise, eight behind him. He has been through something. The Nine of Wands speaks to the exhaustion of someone who has fought hard and is nearly there — but nearly is not yet. It asks for one more push, not from naivety but from the knowledge that the finishing line is close. This is resilience, not martyrdom.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Obstinacy, refusing to give up on something that should be released, or collapsing before the end.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to Strength: inner reserves proving deeper than expected. Next to The Star: the recovery that follows perseverance. Next to The Ten of Wands: the final push before putting down the load.

scenario.love: A relationship requiring more patience and resilience than expected — but worth it.
scenario.work: A long project in its final, exhausting phase. Don't stop now.
scenario.self: Where are you nearly there and don't quite believe it?

currentQuizCards.name: Nine of Pentacles
currentQuizCards.upright: A richly dressed woman stands in a vineyard, a hooded falcon on her wrist, surrounded by abundance she has created. The Nine of Pentacles speaks to the pleasure of self-sufficiency — of having built something through your own effort and enjoying it without apology. In a reading it often signals a period of earned independence and material comfort, and asks whether it is being savoured.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Financial dependence, enjoying abundance that hasn't been earned, or isolation within material success.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Empress: abundance in full expression. Next to The Hermit: the pleasure of one's own company. Next to The Four of Pentacles: comfort that has become isolation.

scenario.love: The independence that makes you a better partner — or the choice to be fully yourself before partnering.
scenario.work: The stage of a career where skill and autonomy intersect.
scenario.self: What have you built that you are not letting yourself enjoy?

currentQuizCards.name: Ten of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: A figure lies face down with ten swords in their back, the sky dark behind them — but the horizon is bright. The Ten of Swords is the card of absolute endings: the defeat that cannot be dressed up, the collapse that is complete. It is one of the most dramatic images in the deck — and it is also always followed by dawn. In a reading it asks you to face what is over fully, so something else can begin.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Resisting an inevitable end, or the recovery that follows hitting bottom.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to Death: a profound and total ending. Next to The Star: dawn genuinely following the darkest point. Next to The Fool: the new beginning that only this ending makes possible.

scenario.love: The end of a relationship — complete, undeniable, painful.
scenario.work: A project, career phase, or organisation that has definitively ended.
scenario.self: What has actually ended that you are still not fully admitting is over?

currentQuizCards.name: Two of Cups
currentQuizCards.upright: Two figures exchange cups before a caduceus topped with a lion's head. This is the card of genuine mutual recognition — romantic or otherwise. Not just attraction but resonance: two people who see each other clearly and choose each other with full awareness. In any context it speaks to a partnership built on real connection and equal exchange.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Imbalance in a relationship, or a connection that seems mutual but isn't. One person more invested than the other.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Lovers: a deep romantic bond with real alignment. Next to The Tower: a partnership under serious pressure. Next to The Devil: attraction that has become compulsive.

scenario.love: A new relationship with genuine potential, or a reconnection with real substance behind it.
scenario.work: A collaboration or business partnership that is genuinely reciprocal.
scenario.self: With whom do you have this kind of mutual recognition, and are you giving it enough attention?

spread.name: Three Card
spread.description: Past, Present, Future — the spine of any reading.

card.name: The High Priestess
card.upright: She sits between two pillars — B and J, Boaz and Jachin, the pillars of Solomon's temple. Duality. She does not speak; she knows. The High Priestess is what cannot be said outright, the knowledge that lives below words. In a reading she asks you to stop analysing and start listening — to dreams, to instinct, to what you already know but won't admit.
card.reversed: Intuition suppressed or ignored. You may know the answer and be refusing it. Reversed, The High Priestess can also point to secrets being withheld by others, or to superficiality replacing depth.
card.relational: Next to The Magician: knowing and doing in balance, or in competition. Next to The Moon: the unconscious is speaking loudly. Next to The Emperor: tension between intuition and rigid structure.

scenario.love: Something unspoken between two people. What is not being said matters more than what is.
scenario.work: Trust the instinct that keeps surfacing. The data may not show what you already sense.
scenario.self: The invitation to go inward before seeking answers outside.

card.name: Nine of Swords
card.upright: A figure sits up in bed, head in hands, nine swords on the wall above them. This is the card of 3am thinking: the anxiety that is worst in the dark, the fears that crowd in when defences are down, the mental anguish that outpaces its actual cause. In a reading the Nine of Swords names the suffering without endorsing the story producing it — and asks whether the worst fears are actually true.
card.reversed: Moving through the darkest period, or anxiety so deep it cannot be named.
card.relational: Next to The Moon: fear and illusion compounding each other. Next to The Star: the night ending. Next to The Four of Swords: rest as the antidote to rumination.

scenario.love: Catastrophic thinking about a relationship — the anxiety that runs ahead of reality.
scenario.work: Worry about a situation that may be far less dire than it feels at night.
scenario.self: Which of the thoughts at 3am are worth examining in daylight, and which are just fear?

card.name: Five of Pentacles
card.upright: Two figures in the snow pass a lit church window — warmth visible but not entered. The Five of Pentacles speaks to material hardship and the particular loneliness of feeling excluded from abundance. In a reading it names financial difficulty, health challenges, or the sense of being left out in the cold. The church window asks: is the help available being sought?
card.reversed: Recovery from financial hardship, or help finally being accepted.
card.relational: Next to The Tower: material collapse. Next to The Star: hardship with hope on the horizon. Next to The Six of Pentacles: help available, if you ask.

scenario.love: Feeling isolated or unsupported in a relationship, or financial strain affecting a partnership.
scenario.work: Financial difficulty, job loss, or a period of professional scarcity.
scenario.self: Where is the door you are not walking through because you don't think you deserve to?

currentReading.querrant: A client has recently been made redundant after 12 years at the same company. They feel lost.

steps.prompt: You look at the spread. The Hierophant sits in the Past position. Before addressing the redundancy directly, what does this card tell you about how they experienced that 12-year period?

options.text: They valued the structure and identity that the institution gave them — perhaps too much.
options.chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

options.text: They were probably too conventional in how they worked — never challenged the rules.
options.chosen_response: That's a possible reading, but it's evaluative rather than curious. The Hierophant in past position more usefully asks: what did that structure mean to them? Judgement about whether they should have challenged it comes later, if at all.

options.text: Religious or cultural values shaped their career choices.
options.chosen_response: Possible, but you're narrowing too fast. The Hierophant's institutional dimension is broader than religion. The more useful question at this stage is: how much of their identity was tied to belonging to that particular institution?

steps._chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

steps.prompt: Death sits in the Present. How do you introduce this card to someone who is already frightened?

options.text: Immediately reassure them that Death never means actual death, then explain the transformation meaning.
options.chosen_response: The reassurance impulse is understandable, but leading with it actually increases anxiety — it signals there's something to be scared of. A better approach: name what is true. Something has ended. That is real, and it matters. Then open toward what becomes possible when something ends.

options.text: Acknowledge that something genuinely has ended, and that grief is the right response — then ask what they are still trying to hold onto.
options.chosen_response: Exactly. Death in the present isn't a threat — it's a description. Something has ended. The grief is real and worth honouring. But the productive question for a reading is: what are they still trying to sustain that has already gone? That's where the actual work lives.

options.text: Focus on transformation — this is a new beginning in disguise.
options.chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps._chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps.prompt: The Star closes the spread in the Future position. After what you've said about the Hierophant and Death, how do you read this?

options.text: Everything will be fine — the Star is a positive card and the future looks good.
options.chosen_response: Too easy. The Star is genuinely hopeful but it isn't a promise of outcome — it's a quality of possibility. Given that the Past shows deep institutional identity and the Present shows real loss, the Star in the future position is asking: can you let yourself hope for something that is genuinely yours, rather than something given to you by an institution?

options.text: Recovery and renewal are available — but the sequence suggests it requires genuinely releasing what's gone, not just waiting it out.
options.chosen_response: Good. The narrative arc across these three cards is: deep institutional belonging → real ending → the possibility of something more authentically one's own. The Star is available, but it follows Death. You can't rush the middle card.

options.text: They should look for work in a field that feels more meaningful and personally chosen.
options.chosen_response: That might be true but you've shifted from reading the cards to giving career advice. The Star speaks to the inner state that makes what comes next possible — renewed hope, trust in the world, a less defended openness. Stay with that.

profile.title: The Initiated Reader
profile.summary: Your instincts are already moving beyond surface symbolism. You understand that position shapes meaning, that cards in combination create narrative, and that the useful question is rarely what a card "means" in isolation.
profile.startPoint: Begin directly with spread practice. The Minor Arcana will be your focus — you don't need the Major Arcana foundations.

currentQuizFeedback.good: Well spotted. Wands — The suit emblem of fire — will, creativity, and enterprise. A living branch, always sprouting leaves: energy that grows as it burns. At this level it also appears on: Ace of Wands, Two of Wands, Three of Wands, Four of Wands.
currentQuizFeedback.bad: Look again — the card carries this one: Wands — The suit emblem of fire — will, creativity, and enterprise. A living branch, always sprouting leaves: energy that grows as it burns. At this level it also appears on: Ace of Wands, Two of Wands, Three of Wands, Four of Wands.
